notes.note: The letter-heading; addressed to P. Silius (Nerva), propraetor of Bithynia and Pontus in 51-50 BC, the recipient of several recommendations from Cicero during his own Cilician proconsulship. The man recommended here, Egnatius, is one Silius already holds dear.

notes.anchor: That undertaking of ours has, of course, fallen through.
notes.note: An oblique reference to some shared project or plan (illa nostra) that has come to nothing; Cicero does not specify it, promising to explain in person. The exact undertaking is unknown to us.

notes.anchor: ``What if this is for the better?''
notes.note: Cicero quotes a proverbial "common consolation" (vulgaris consolatio): the thought that an apparent setback may prove to be for the best. The framing as a stock saying suggests a proverb or a tag from comedy rather than Cicero's own coinage.
